ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for youtiao; mapped to the closest parent class 'fried dough food product' (FOODON_03302050). Exact youtiao entry not present in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Doughnuts and similar fried dough products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers doughnuts and similar fried dough products; used as the closest defensible analogue for youtiao. No dedicated FoodEx2 entry for youtiao exists as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for youtiao or generic fried dough fritters as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of youtiao is one stick weighing approximately 50 grams, providing around 193 kcal, commonly consumed alongside soya bean milk or congee at breakfast.

nutrient_source.title: Cruller, fried dough (Chinese-style) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Chinese-style fried dough (cruller). Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB nutrient database for regional alignment. Input enriched_json values were consistent with USDA data and have been retained where within acceptable variance. Saturated fat estimated from USDA total fat profile for deep-fried wheat dough products.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for youtiao found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 74 estimated using white bread / refined wheat flour deep-fried dough analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Refined wheat flour dough products deep-fried in oil consistently show GI in the 70–76 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (74 × 17.25) / 100 = 12.8g per 50g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100g) and high saturated fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid youtiao due to its high sodium content (450 mg/100g) and significant fat load from deep-frying, both of which contribute to elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular strain.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~74) from refined wheat flour and rapid postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit youtiao, as its high GI (~74) and refined carbohydrate content (34.5g/100g) cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; the fat content provides only modest glycemic attenuation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (24.5g/100g), saturated fat (3.8g/100g), and elevated sodium from deep-frying in vegetable oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of youtiao due to its high total fat, saturated fat, and sodium content, all of which are associated with increased LDL cholesterol, arterial stiffness, and elevated cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile from deep-frying and refined flour
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit youtiao as deep-fried refined flour products are associated with pro-inflammatory dietary patterns that may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Youtiao (油条), commonly known as Chinese fried dough fritters or Chinese doughnuts, are long golden-brown strips of leavened wheat dough deep-fried in vegetable oil until puffed, airy, and crisp. The dough is prepared from plain wheat flour, water, salt, baking powder, and baking soda; two strips are traditionally pressed together before frying, producing a characteristic twin-stick shape with a porous, chewy interior and a lightly crispy, slightly salty exterior. Youtiao are a staple of Chinese breakfasts across mainland China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and throughout Southeast Asia, where they are known as Cakoi in Malay-speaking communities. They are most commonly served alongside warm soya bean milk (dipped or dunked), congee (rice porridge), or as a crunchy garnish in Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup) and Rojak (spiced fruit salad). Nutritionally, youtiao are energy-dense at approximately 385 kcal per 100g, with a high fat content (24.5g/100g) dominated by monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from frying oil, moderate protein (6.5g/100g) from wheat gluten, and a significant refined carbohydrate load (34.5g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.2g/100g). Sodium is notably high at 450 mg/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 74 (high), making youtiao a food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. Clinically, youtiao is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high GI, sodium, and fat profile. It is vegetarian and vegan-friendly and is free from dairy and cholesterol when fried in vegetable oil.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a youtiao?
What is the GI of youtiao?
What do you eat with youtiao?
How much sodium is in youtiao?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for Chinese-style fried dough and have been retained from the input enriched_json after cross-validation. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, water, and cholesterol values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy profiles for deep-fried wheat dough products. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for refined wheat flour deep-fried dough products; no direct youtiao GI measurement was found in the approved database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (13.5g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (5.8g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.0g/100g), cholesterol (0.0g/100g), and water (33.0g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for Chinese-style fried dough cruller (NDB No. 18033) as these detailed fatty acid and moisture values were not present in the input enriched_json. 2. Vitamin A (0.0 mcg RAE), vitamin C (0.0 mg), vitamin D (0.0 mcg), and vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg) are reported as zero; these are confirmed negligible/zero values consistent with a plant-based deep-fried wheat dough product with no animal-derived ingredients, per USDA FoodData Central. 3. GI value of 74 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for youtiao was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2024. The estimate is based on refined wheat flour deep-fried dough analogues. 4. The input portions array contained two identical entries (both 50g, both is_default: true); the duplicate has been resolved to a single canonical 50g serving. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact codes for youtiao exist in these ontologies as of 2024. 6. Tamil and Hindi local names are not established in standard reference sources for this food item and have been left null; youtiao is not a traditional Tamil or Hindi-named food.
